data.title: I'm Raising Cash: 5 Warning Signs Of A Market Bubble Are Here
data.description:
The stock market has been in a clear bull market, and it recently hit new all-time highs. However, there are some signs that not all is well when you take a deeper look at what has been driving this rally and some warning signs that are looming. It is difficult to take your chips off the table, especially when you and almost everyone else is making money. But this is what disciplined investors need to consider doing because I believe the risks of being highly or fully invested in this stock market are growing. Let's take a closer look below at some of these concerns:

The Chart

As the chart below shows, the S&P 500 Index (NYSEARCA:SPY) is trading at or near all-time highs. This 5-year chart shows how far this market has come and that it has experienced a massive rebound from the April stock market lows, which was due to concerns about Liberation Day Tariffs. When I look at this chart, it doesn't look to me like this is a time to invest in the stock market, because the market is overbought and so extended now that a correction could be looming.

1) Stock Ownership Is At Levels Seen Just Before The Great Financial Crisis

According to recent reports, about 62% of U.S. households now own stocks. This might seem like a positive, but historically, this is not the case because when stock ownership is at high levels, it is often a sign of a market top. The last time stock ownership hit the 62% level was back in 2007, just before the Great Financial Crisis. Stock ownership dropped to around 52% of households in 2013 and 2016. The peak for stock ownership was in 2004 at 63%, but just seeing that we are back at levels not seen since just before the Great Financial Crisis is a potentially significant warning sign. Stocks are popular during bull markets and unpopular after investors see losses in bear markets. We are clearly in a bull market and possibly at or near a peak in the popularity of stocks.

2) The Breadth Of This Stock Market Is Not Healthy And Bubble-Like

The market is being mostly carried by just a handful of stocks, and one of the biggest contributors are mega-cap tech stocks. The problem is that many of these companies have announced enormous capex spending plans for AI, and that has created a frenzy for AI-related stocks and tech stocks in general. The total capex spending by the Magnificent Seven is estimated to be around $368 billion in 2025. Without this spending and the subsequent rally in tech stocks, the stock market would not be doing anywhere near as well in 2025. The amount of money being thrown at AI capex seems like an arms race, and the problem with an arms race is that at some point, someone is going to lose, and when they do lose, they will curtail or end their capex spending.

The bulls suggest there is a chance that other sectors (besides tech) will play catch-up and start to show more strength. This would improve the breadth, but the stock market is already at historically high levels, so while this is possible, already rich valuations could limit this possibility. The dot-com bubble experienced narrowing market breadth just before the bubble burst, so the narrow breadth of the market today is a potential warning sign if it does not improve soon.

There is another reason why narrow market breadth is so concerning, and that’s because stocks like Nvidia (NVDA) have ballooned in value, so much so that it now represents about 8% of the S&P 500 Index. This is an unprecedented level of concentration in a major index that has 499 other stocks in it. This concentration risk is huge, and it is even worse because Nvidia reported in Q2 that just 2 customers represented 39% of its revenues for that quarter. With Nvidia being about 8% of the S&P 500 Index, the stock market is highly dependent on Nvidia, and with just 2 customers representing 39% of its quarterly revenues, that means Nvidia is highly dependent on what 2 customers do going forward. If these 2 companies reduce spending with Nvidia, it could seriously impact its revenues, and that could seriously impact the entire stock market since Nvidia is such a huge weighting.

3) Bubble-Like Valuations from Bitcoin To AI Stocks

Bubble-like valuations can be seen in everything from the shares of a major retailer to Bitcoin to AI stocks and more. Extremely high valuations in many areas are often viewed in hindsight (after a major correction or bubble popping) as having been obvious warning signs. But, during bubble-like periods, investors convince themselves that these valuations are the new normal and that this time it is different. It is easy to be convinced that an asset is priced at the right level if others are willing to pay that price. However, we know from history that prices are not always sustainable, and many investors have learned that lesson the hard way.

Costco (COST) is trading for around 50 times earnings estimates.

Palantir (PLTR) is now trading for about 260 times earnings estimates.

Tesla (TSLA) has recently surged and now trades for about 250 times earnings estimates.

4) Speculative Segments That Have Marked The Top In The Past Are Popular Once Again

Special Purpose Acquisition Companies, or "SPACs," are making a comeback in 2025, and we know how that ended the last time these became popular. IPO activity is booming in 2025, to levels not seen in ten years. We know that strong IPO activity occurs during bull markets and peaks at market tops and dries up at market lows. Investors have to ask themselves if they want to be buying at or near a market top when it is fun or buy at market lows when buying stocks is the last thing many investors want to do.

5) Valuation Metrics Are Now At Or Exceeding Past Market Tops

The cyclically adjusted price-to-earnings ratio, or "CAPE," is currently at 37. This is historically high since the CAPE ratio is about 17, on average. Warren Buffett uses a well-known metric called the "Buffett Indicator," which takes the total value of the U.S. stock market and divides it by the Gross Domestic Product, or "GDP." The current estimated value of the U.S. stock market is near $65 trillion, and the U.S. GDP is nearly $30 trillion. This indicates a 216% ratio of market value to GDP, which is unprecedented, and it suggests overvaluation. By looking at the chart below, it is clear that the valuation of the U.S. stock market has surged in the past few years, and it might be at unsustainable levels.

Where I Could Be Wrong

I believe there are clearly many signs of a bubble in the stock market; however, bubbles can get bigger and last longer than many people expect. So, the market could easily rally further, and I plan to benefit if it does, because I am keeping my core positions, but I do believe caution is warranted. As the saying goes, "Don't fight the Fed," so I might be mistaken to sell some stocks into this market rally since the Fed appears to be signaling a new round of easing. Capex spending on AI still appears strong, so if this continues, it could add more fuel to the rally.

In Summary

Asset bubbles have a history of popping sooner or later, and when they do, it can be sudden and often catches many investors off-guard. I believe that even if this bubble-like market has more room to run, it is likely due for a pullback, if not a more significant correction.

I believe being contrarian and being disciplined by raising cash now and waiting for better buying opportunities could be a rewarding strategy that also lets you sleep well at night.

data.title: Invest Smartly: Take A Look At KKR's KKRS And KKRT
data.description:
Co-authored by Strategic Yield.

Today, we present another possible choice with less risk. The focus is on KKR & Co. Inc. (NYSE:KKR) and its baby bonds: (KKRS) and (NYSE:KKRT).

Company Overview

We start, as always, with a few words for the company. So, founded in 1976, KKR is a global investment firm with industry-leading investment experience, with over 3000 employees and approximately 28 offices across 4 continents. The company has a current market capitalization of close to $133 billion. The total assets at the end of the second quarter of 2025 are approximately $380.8 billion. KKR has a BBB+ rating from S&P and an A from Fitch, which puts it in the "investment grade" category.

Below, you can see the company's industry diversification and strategy:

KKR invests in a broad range of sectors, including business services, healthcare, and infrastructure, which reduces concentration risk.

Below, we can see a total return comparison between KKR and SPY, and how KKR outperforms:

3-year total return:

5-year total return:

And here is a 5-year total return with its peers:

The common stock of the company is now traded at $149.34:

KKR has maintained 14 years of continuous dividend payments, the last 5 of which have been in increasing amounts. Here is the dividend history:

The total dividend for the last 12 months is $0.74, and the dividend yield is around 0.5%.

Below, you can observe the forward estimations:

Baby Bonds

KKRT is trading above par, $25.80 currently, with a current yield of 6.67% and a yield to worst of 6.37%. The call date is 06/01/2030 (about 4.7 years away), with a yield to call of 6.37%. The maturity is 39.7 years, with a yield to maturity of 6.85%. It was issued on 05/28/2025 with 22 million shares at a par value of $25 each, generating $550 million in gross proceeds. It is investment grade, rated BBB+ by Fitch and BBB+ by S&P. It pays an interest distribution of 6.88% per annum, or $1.71875. KKRT offers a spread of about 2.1% over the 30-year treasury yield.

KKRS is currently priced at $19, below par, with a current yield of 6.08%. It has close to 0.5 years to call, with a call date of 04/01/2026. The maturity date is on 04/01/2061, with 35.53 years to maturity and 6.46% yield to maturity. It was issued on 03/25/2021 with 20 million shares at a par value of $25 each, generating $500 million in gross proceeds. It is investment grade, rated BBB+ by Fitch and BBB+ by S&P. It pays an interest distribution of 4.63% per annum, or $1.15625, in the form of $0.2890625 per quarter. KKRS offers a spread of about 1.7% over the 30-year treasury yield, which compensates investors for the additional risk.

Disclosure: For those wondering how we calculate the yields, we calculate the internal rate of return for a series of cash flows. We include an initial investment (or current price), periodic payments, and a final payment at maturity, which allows us to calculate XIRR.

Below we can see a comparison between KKRS and its peers in the finance sector, trade below par, and those that have investment-grade credit ratings with their yields to worst:

And here is a comparison between KKRT (at the top) and its peers in the finance sector, traded above par, investment-grade, with their yields to worst:

OTC bonds

The table above shows KKR's OTC bonds with their characteristics. Their yield range is from 4.5% to 5.6%, but they are not rated by the major credit agencies. We prefer stocks that have a higher yield and have investment-grade credit ratings.

Investment Risks

Investing in KKRS and KKRT offers attractive returns but carries risks. Changes in interest rates may affect bond prices. Market volatility in the financial sector may reduce returns. The long maturity and high duration of the bonds expose investors to long-term market risks, and economic instability may affect KKR's ability to maintain stable payments.

Conclusion

KKRS and KKRT offer attractive yields, with an investment-grade rating, making them suitable for conservative investors seeking stable income. Despite the risks of interest rate changes and market volatility, KKR’s strong diversification and $380.8 billion in assets support reliability.

data.title: Q2 Earnings Review: An 'Expensive' Quarter For WhiteFiber
data.description:
Back in August, Bit Digital (BTBT) spun out its HPC/AI subsidiary business, WhiteFiber (NASDAQ:WYFI), through an IPO at $17 per share. The company recently released its first quarterly report as a standalone public entity for the three months that ended June 2025. The market likes what it sees, with shares eclipsing $28 on September 19th. In this initial coverage of WYFI, we'll look at that earnings report, equity valuation, and assess whether investors should consider WYFI shares over BTU shares.

Q2 Earnings

3 Months Ended June 2025 2024 YoY
Revenue $18,662,249 $12,589,701 48.2%
COGS -$7,201,168 -$4,595,301 56.7%
Depreciation/Amortization -$5,140,713 -$4,322,291 18.9%
General & Admin -$15,476,832 -$1,261,489 1126.9%
Total Opex -$27,818,713 -$10,179,081 173.3%
Loss From Operations -$9,156,464 $2,410,620 -479.8%

Source: WhiteFiber 10-Q

For the quarter ended June 2025, WhiteFiber reported $18.7 million in top-line revenue. This was a 48% year-over-year increase for the quarter and a 70% increase for the first half of 2025 over H1 2024. Notably, COGS grew faster than Revenue and G&A exploded year-over-year by 1,100% in Q2.

6 Months Ended 2025 2024 YoY
Revenue $35,423,511 $20,759,033 70.6%
COGS -$13,818,011 -$7,752,628 78.2%

Depreciation/Amortization

 -$8,970,357 -$7,203,818 24.5%
General & Admin -$19,754,485 -$2,458,408 703.5%
Total Opex -$42,542,853 -$17,414,854 144.3%

Loss From Operations

 -$7,119,342 $3,344,179 -312.9%

Source: WhiteFiber 10-Q

I think the most telling thing in these tables is the fact that WhiteFiber has actually flipped from a profitable operator in the first half of 2024 to one that lost over $7 million through the first half of 2025. That loss is entirely attributable to an expensive Q2, as WhiteFiber was profitable in Q1.

Breaking out the revenue by segment, the overwhelming majority of WhiteFiber's revenue comes from cloud services, with that segment accounting for $16.6 million, or 91% of the company's top line:

$ in Millions Q1-25 Q1-24 YoY
Cloud Services $16.6 $12.5 32.8%
Co-location $1.7 $0.0 N/A
Cloud % of Total 90.7% 100.0%

Source: WhiteFiber 10-Q

Last year the company didn't have a co-location segment and acquired one through the purchase of Enovum for about $46 million. The cloud services segment uses leased GPUs to support generative AI applications.

That said, GPU lease expense actually came down in the quarter. From where I sit, the biggest operational expense shareholders should keep an eye on going forward is the G&A. The large spike in general and administrative expense in Q2 was due to $6.5 million in share-based compensation, $1.3 million in salaries and bonuses, as well as $5.7 million in consulting expenses.

Balance Sheet and Valuation

Shifting to the balance sheet, at the end of the quarter, WhiteFiber had $16.4 million in cash and $50.3 million in current assets. The company's equity was mainly derived from its property, plant and equipment of $230 million:

Keeping in mind that the IPO happened after this quarter ended, WYFI likely has roughly $200 million in cash and equivalents as of article submission. Total Liabilities came in at $78.3 million at the end of June, with nearly $38 million of those liabilities being non-current lease liabilities. We'll get a better sense of the company's balance sheet position in the next report. But given Bit Digital's balance sheet management history, I would assume WhiteFiber is in good financial shape.

If we look at Bit Digital's valuation through an annualized P/S multiple, the company is now trading in line with 'AI/HPC' peers like CoreWeave (CRWV) or Core Scientific (CORZ). At an 11.5x forward sales multiple, the company is clearly priced for growth. There's certainly nothing wrong with that. And frankly, I think WYFI is one of the market's better AI/HPC pure plays. However, for those who want WYFI at a cheaper entry, there may be an alternative stock that makes more sense today.

Bit Digital Ownership

After spinning out WhiteFiber through IPO, Bit Digital retained the overwhelming majority of WYFI shares. Currently, Bit Digital owns about 71.5% of WYFI shares. What I find so interesting about the post-earnings explosion in the price of WYFI is that it isn't benefiting BTBT at all so far.

In fact, over the last month, WYFI shares are up by almost 50%, while BTBT shares have appreciated by less than 8%. This would perhaps be understandable if Bit Digital's WYFI position wasn't a large component of the company's total assets. Yet, the opposite is true:

Bit Digital Holdings Units Unit Value Total Value
Ethereum 121,252 $4,484.58 $543,764,294
WYFI Shares 27,043,750 $28.00 $757,225,000
Combined value $1,300,989,294

Not only does Bit Digital hold more WYFI than it does Ethereum (ETH-USD) at current prices for each asset, but at a $1 billion market capitalization, BTBT shares are trading at nearly a 30% discount to the ETH and WYFI that shareholders of BTBT would own. Factoring in the $181 million in cash and $90 million in total liabilities Bit Digital had at the end of Q2, buying BTBT instead of WYFI is almost like acquiring WYFI and cash and getting the ETH nearly for free.

Closing Summary

I think WYFI is a great trading asset at this point because it has a relatively low float and has been very volatile in its brief trading history. I have held WYFI in the past and will likely hold it again in the future. Fundamentally, I'd like to see the company return to operational profitability before getting overly exposed in a direct investment position. For me, the spike in G&A expense is a concern and something to watch in future earnings reports. Today, I like the idea of holding BTBT as a WYFI proxy because I'm getting the WYFI shares and the ETH for less than market value. I'm going to initiate WYFI with a cautious 'buy' recommendation. But again, I'm planning to wait for this post-earnings moonshot to settle down before I have any significant direct position.

data.title: Equinor's Orsted Investment Is Intelligent
data.description:
Equinor ASA (NYSE: NYSE:EQNR) is a major oil company that has been a prominent investor in renewable power. The company has seen its share since we recommended investing in it, with a number of factors, including the struggles faced by Ørsted A/S. Ørsted is a major wind producer which Equinor has a 10% ownership stake. Despite that, Equinor remains a valuable investment.

Ørsted and Revolution

Ørsted owns the Revolution Wind project, which the Trump administration halted at 80% complete, after billions of dollars in capital was spent.

The net result was Ørsted diluting shareholders substantially, with 900 million new shares being added to its existing share count of more than 400 million. Part of the reason for this capital raise is the company's inability to farm-out its Sunrise Wind project off the coast of NYC, another American project that faces cancellation threats.

However, not only does Ørsted have these projects, but Equinor also has Empire Wind off the New York coast it's working to complete. The company is suing the Trump administration over Revolution Wind, and it could get a more favorable administration in a few years, so the outcome here remains to be seen.

The upside though is that Equinor gets access to a major offshore wind company with substantial renewable energy production and continued growth.

As seen above, Equinor has substantially grown its renewable power generation while focusing less on less long-term gas-to-power generation. However, through the company's Ørsted stake it now has 1.9 GW of capacity that is expected to grow to 2.7 GW in the next few years. The company had 9.1 TWh in 1H 2025 production, or roughly 4.6 TWh per quarter.

That means that Equinor's 10% stake gives it 50% extra renewable production. That can grow by another 50% over the next few years.

Equinor Discoveries

Equinor has made a number of impressive discoveries recently.

This includes up to 1.1 million cubic meters of natural gas, a minor discovery that adds up with the company's other discoveries. Equinor also recently officially opened the Johan Castberg field, Norway's northernmost oil field. The field is expected to produce for at least 30 years with a massive 220k barrels / day of production.

These new discoveries and production will be key to the company's future success.

Equinor Portfolio

Equinor's overall production remains strong.

The company had more than 2 million barrels / day in equity oil and gas production. That makes the company a strong and valuable investment, as it continues to focus on low carbon production. It's also worth noting here, that Equinor's natural gas production is essential to Europe where the company earns strong returns as Russian production is replaced.

The company continues to have a strong power portfolio as well. Equinor had 0.83 TWh in its share of renewable power. Renewable power isn't super expensive, a TWh is roughly $100 million, much smaller than the company's oil and gas portfolio.

Equinor Shareholder Returns

Financially, the company has lofty tax obligations, but it's continuing to focus on shareholder returns.

Equinor had $5.7 billion in net operating income in the most recent quarter with $6.5 billion in adjusted operating income. The company is in a stable but high tax environment, with $1.94 billion in CFFO after taxes paid. The company had $0.64 in adjusted earnings per share, putting it at a high single-digit P/E ratio.

The company has continued to maintain its lofty dividend that it can comfortably afford that comes out to roughly 6%. The company is also committed to its $5 billion share buyback program that's 8% of its market cap, which means comfortable double-digit shareholder returns that Equinor can comfortably afford.

Those share buybacks enable the company to save on dividend expenses going forward, while increasing the value of its assets.

Thesis Risk

The largest risk to our thesis is crude oil prices (CL1:COM). Equinor is still primarily an oil and gas company that's driven by oil and natural gas prices. That means that any substantial price weakness, from a tariff induced recession for example, could substantially hurt the company's ability to continue driving shareholder returns.

Conclusion

Equinor has an impressive oil and natural gas production that forms the core of its business and shareholder returns. The company is guiding for a 6% dividend yield and an even higher % share buyback ratio. That will enable the company to save even more on dividends and it will enable the core of the company's portfolio.

At the same time, the company is doubling-down on its 10% stake in Ørsted. That will enable the company to expand its renewables portfolio providing more valuable cash flow worth hundreds of millions annually and strong diversity for the company's oil and gas portfolio. That combination makes Equinor a valuable investment.

Let us know your thoughts in the comments below.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: AMD: Priced Like Fundamentals Are Flawless
data.description:
Investment thesis

My pessimism about the strength of Advanced Micro Devices, Inc.'s (NASDAQ:AMD) prospects in the AI era that I have shared throughout 2024 aged quite well. However, my previous bearish call on AMD did not age well because the stock gained around 33% since early June. I think that this rally was more based on emotions rather than relying on fundamentals. As a result, AMD's valuation currently mirrors Nvidia's despite having quite questionable strategic positioning. In my previous articles on AMD, I have already highlighted its significant gap with Nvidia. This time I want to emphasize that the space is becoming more crowded, and Nvidia is currently not the only competitive threat, in my opinion. The Q2 performance also raises questions regarding the strength of AMD's business mix, as the latest quarterly performance was quite volatile across the company's segments. All in all, reiterating "Strong Sell" does not look unfairly pessimistic.

Fundamentals and valuation diverge

AMD released its latest quarterly earnings on Aug. 5 with a modest revenue beat and in-line adjusted EPS. The Q2 revenue growth was solid, a 31.7% YoY increase. However, if we look deeper than just consolidated revenue growth, there are some warning signs.

AMD announced its earnings more than a month ago and it's the main reason why I do not want to make it an in-depth earnings review of AMD. Therefore, I just want to emphasize that the strength of AMD's business mix is questionable, as the performance across segments was very volatile, as suggested by the slide below.

I'm not very optimistic about soaring Client & Gaming revenue with 69% YoY growth just because it's highly likely explained by seasonality. The by-segment revenue breakdown from the Q2 2023 earnings presentation suggests that in Q2 2022 this business (which was two separate segments at that time) generated slightly higher revenue of $3.8 billion ($2.2 billion in Client + $1.6 billion in Gaming). The compilation below of this segment's revenue trajectory reflects the average three-year PC replacement cycle with a sharp revenue decline in Q2 2024-2023. Thus, the segment's soaring Q2 2025 segment revenue is highly likely just another peak of the PC replacement cycle. Therefore, we're likely to see the segment's revenue showing weakness again in 2026-2027. It's also crucial to say that despite the segment's revenue rebound in Q2 2025, its operating margin declined compared to Q2 2022. This might indicate that AMD's offerings in this segment are losing their attractiveness to customers, which undermines pricing power.

Furthermore, the Embedded segment continued struggling with a 4% YoY revenue decline and operating income dropping much faster. I would like to remind you that AMD acquired this business (Xilinx) less than four years ago and paid a staggering $50 billion for it. If we also compare the Q2 2025 performance not on a YoY basis but compared to Q2 2023, the performance of the segment is staggeringly disappointing. As we can see, the Embedded segment's revenue dropped from almost $1.5 billion to $0.8 billion within just two years. This dynamic does not add much optimism, especially if we recall how expensive this acquisition was and the fact that AMD's goodwill skyrocketed from around $300 million to $24 billion as a result of the deal. As a former auditor, I think it's quite probable that this goodwill might not sustain the impairment test in the foreseeable future given the segment's declining performance, leading to potential impairment losses and downward pressure on the EPS.

Now, the biggest growth driver of the past few years has been the Data Center business. Since Nvidia is the biggest rival and benchmark for AMD in this segment, I think that making a comparative analysis is a very good idea. Moreover, NVDA's Q2 earnings are not that old as they were released in late August. AMD's Data Center revenue grew by 14% YoY in Q2, peanuts compared to Nvidia's 56% YoY sales spike in this segment.

Of course, some people might say that comparing AMD's revenue to Nvidia's might be kind of "apples to oranges" because the latter's data center revenue is higher than AMD's by almost 13 times. This is a fair comment on the one hand. On the other hand, let's not forget that AMD's and Nvidia's scale was approximately in the same ballpark for ages, and AI (mostly Data Center business) was the real game changer that helped Nvidia become miles ahead of AMD. Therefore, the comparison does make sense and literally screams about AMD's inability to compete with Nvidia in data centers.

And the worst part for AMD's bulls here is that competitive threats in this business are intensifying because of the massive multi-trillion AI infrastructure market potential. Other prominent tech players do not want to miss such a great opportunity, and the list of new threats should really make AMD's management worry about the company's prospects to capitalize on AI tailwinds.

First, there's another semiconductor champion, Broadcom Inc. (AVGO), which aggressively expands its AI exposure getting new multi-billion orders to supply their cutting-edge XPUs. Second, cloud behemoths like Google and Amazon certainly want to be more cost-efficient, and seeing how Nvidia's shareholder wealth is growing exponentially, it's no surprise that they are working hard on developing in-house data center processors. Third, let's not forget that there are smaller but nevertheless quite strong semiconductor players like QUALCOMM Incorporated (QCOM) ready to return to the data center business. Last but not least, there's always a potential threat from Chinese tech giants.

Thus, it's obvious that it's not only AMD vs. Nvidia in the data center business anymore. The competition is becoming significantly more intense, and new players are not just some cash burning ambitious startups that have 50-50 chances to survive. Instead, all new competitors are extremely dangerous competitors with vast financial resources and the ability to headhunt the industry's brightest engineers. In this situation, it will be extremely challenging for AMD to protect shareholder value. Most of the company's TTM profitability metrics are already lower than historical averages despite revenue growth. Some key metrics, like FCF or EBIT margin, are already in single digits, which is a very vulnerable situation, leaving limited room for ramping up investments in growth. Therefore, AMD's competitive positioning looks quite vulnerable.

Therefore, I think that AMD's sky-high P/E ratio is absolutely not justified. Most of AMD's valuation ratios are by far higher than the sector median. In addition, AMD's forward P/E ratios are approximately in line with Nvidia's, which does not look fair given the massive gap in the strength of the fundamentals of these two companies.

AMD's normalized earnings yield of just above one also screams overvaluation. The company's last five years' average is around 40% higher, which is a massive gap. In addition, Nvidia's normalized earnings yield is closer to 2, which once again says that AMD is unfairly valued compared to its much stronger, strategically positioned rival.

Finally, AMD is a very weak performer during the last four months of the year from a seasonality perspective. Over the past decade, September, October, and December have demonstrated extremely low win rates well below 50%.

Risks update

AMD enjoys strong share price momentum, which is usually a robust near-term catalyst. AMD is far ahead of the S&P 500 Index in terms of share price performance over the last six months, which might add a lot of optimism and contribute to positive sentiment around the stock.

Share price strength might also be supported by optimism from Wall Street analysts. Apart from the average "Buy" rating from them, their average target price is also 16.7% higher than the last close.

Bottom line

To conclude, I believe AMD presents a clear "Strong Sell" opportunity. The business mix appears vulnerable, and the market seems to be misled by headline Q2 earnings numbers alone. However, when we delve deeper into the analysis, several significant red flags emerge.

data.title: Marvell: One Of The Last Undervalued AI Names
data.description:
Investment Thesis

AI is undoubtedly the darling of the decade and for good reasons. After the amazing growth that we experienced in the last 2-3 years, AI is still estimated to grow at a CAGR of 27.7% through 2030, with even higher estimates coming from other sources. This is due to AI’s potential to automate tasks, increase efficiency, and improve decision-making.

I wrote several articles about AI companies, and I had many positions (and still have some) in both hardware and software. But today it’s very difficult to find an undervalued name for a new position. One area that was left a bit behind is Data Center connectivity, and I recently wrote bullish articles about Coherent (COHR) and about POET Technologies (POET) related to this niche.

Perhaps an even interesting name is Marvell Technology, Inc. (NASDAQ:MRVL), because it developed an economic moat, something that Coherent did not have (while POET is still a pre-revenue company). Of course, growth is there, but is it good value too? Is Marvell a GARP (Growth At a Reasonable Price) stock?

Business Analysis

Marvell is a leading supplier of data infrastructure semiconductor solutions, with the following business segments, or end markets:

They have a firm focus on data centers, with this segment constantly growing and reaching today ~75% of total revenues.

From another point of view, their main product classes are:

  • Compute, focused on XPU (Custom AI accelerators), with interfaces like their 2nm 64Gbps die-to-die interconnect, and integrating CPO (co-packaged optics), with their 6.4T 3D SiPho engine. CPO is a must for increasing speeds in scale-up architectures.
  • Opticals, with different products such as PAM optical DSPs (digital signal processors) for up to 1.6T optical transceivers, for scale-up architectures; pluggable optical modules and coherent DSPs for up to 800G pluggable modules, for longer distances; coherent-lite DSPs for intermediate distances.
  • Network switches, supporting both open standard Ethernet and UALink.
  • Storage, with storage controllers and accelerators and DPU (Data Processing Units) for data centers, carrier 5G infrastructure, enterprise clients, but also with QLogic Fibre Channel – their solution for storage area networks.

Although they don’t clearly separate sales into these product classes, we have a comment from management that “Since Q4, where optics was about half, custom was about a quarter and then other was about 25%. Obviously, optics and custom have both come up since then. “

And a final point of view is geographical, with a concerning dependence on China:

However, please notice that this is not necessarily indicative of the geographic location of the company’s end customers, as stated in their 10-Q: “For example, a substantial majority of the shipments made to China relate to sales to non-China based customers that have factories or contract manufacturing operations located within China.”

Economic Moat and Market Size

Classification by product class is especially useful in determining Marvell’s economic moat:

Custom ASIC

Broadcom (AVGO) has a market share of 55% to 60%, while Marvell ranks second with a share of 13% to 15% (and increasing). Their multi-year partnerships like the five-year agreement with AWS, or the partnership to integrate NVIDIA’s proprietary NVLink Fusion interconnect platform into Marvell’s custom silicon offerings, are the base for an economic moat (maybe narrow for now, but growing).

Opticals

Marvell is a leader in coherent DSP, together with Acacia/Cisco and Ciena, with their DSP being used by Coherent or Lumentum. They also received Lightwave innovation honors in 2024 for PAM DSP, coherent DSP and pluggable modules. However, I don’t think that they have an edge in pluggable optical modules, with a crowdy market (which I described in my article about Coherent).

Network Switches

It seems that Broadcom has ~90% market share for data centers, while Cisco (CSCO) is leader for the global Ethernet market, with ~32% market share. I see less reasons for an economic moat for Marvell.

DPU

While I couldn’t find a market share, competition is also strong.

Overall, we could say a narrow economic moat for about half of Marvell’s business. However, another source of moat comes from cross-sell opportunities, with Marvell’s integrated platform (compute, connectivity, storage). Therefore, I would say that Marvell deserves a narrow economic moat.

Ultimately, an economic moat translates into ROIC (Return on Invested Capital) in excess of WACC (Weighted Average Cost of Capital), and Marvell does not score well here. However, it seems clear that this is a deliberate move for now, with huge R&D expenses (about 27% margin) to gain market share.

And indeed, it seems that Marvell performs well on this front, with 18 new projects touted after their last earnings call (and some additional ones in the meantime). Based on these, the company seeks to grow their data center market share from 13% of a $33B TAM in calendar '24 to 20% of a $94B TAM in calendar '28:

That $55.4B for Custom Compute in 2028 seems a little optimistic to me. From other sources we can find:

  • Custom ASIC: ~$38B in 2028, with even lower estimates from other sources.
  • Data Center Switches: ~$13B in 2028, in line with company’s estimates.
  • Data Center Interconnect: ~$19B in 2028, in line with company’s estimates.
  • DPU close to $3B in 2028, so $6.5B in line with company’s estimates.

In total we have Data Center TAM ~76.5B in 2028, let’s stick with that prior $75B estimate from Figure #3 for a more pessimistic model.

Financial Analysis

We already saw their revenues for the first six months (see Figure #1), and here is the outlook for Q3 (please keep in mind that Marvell divested its automotive business):

We expect momentum to continue in the third quarter, with total company revenue forecast at $2.06 billion at the midpoint, representing 36% year-over-year growth. Excluding revenue from automotive Ethernet, the implied revenue growth for Marvell's go-forward business would be closer to 40% year-over-year at the midpoint of our forecast for the third quarter.

GAAP gross margin was 50.4% in the last quarter and is expected to be between 51.5% and 52% in Q3. I will model 51% for the entire year, then slightly increasing with higher scale.

R&D had a pattern of 3%-6% growth in the last years, and I expect this to continue, due to high R&D required for their custom chips. As mentioned, R&D margin is already at very elevated levels, about 27%, but with growth lower than sales growth, this margin is expected to gradually decrease over time.

For SG&A I expect an even lower growth, if at all, coming especially from lower G&A (it actually decreased in the last year due to lower amortization expenses for acquired intangible assets).

And if we already saw their mid-term expectations for the Data Center segment (see Figure #3), the company's expectations for the other segments (which will be grouped in “Communication and Other”) are:

  • Enterprise networking and carrier infrastructure: ~$2B annual revenue over time.
  • Consumer: $300M annual revenue.
  • Industrial: $100M annual revenue.

For an optimistic model, I will use 19% market share instead of 20% seen by the company from the $94B Data Center TAM (see Figure #3). I will also consider higher R&D and S&M growth to achieve these bold goals. My 2028 estimates are:

We can see that consensus is closer to my pessimistic model, with $13B estimated revenue for Jan’29:

Finally, looking at their balance sheet, Marvell had total debt of $4.5B at the end of Q2, with improved gross debt-to-EBITDA ratio of ~1.63x and net debt-to-EBITDA ratio of ~1.19x. This improvement was also acknowledged by Fitch which upgraded their credit rating from BBB- to BBB.

The divestiture of the automotive ethernet business for $2.5B gave them flexibility to continue their ongoing stock repurchase program (they have repurchased $540M of stock through the first half of the fiscal year, with ~$2B remaining, a good sign since we see total shares outstanding a little down) and deploy capital to grow their technology platform.

Fair Value

For a relative valuation, we can look at Marvell’s five-year averages. However, for every industry there are some particularities, and in this case, I find only growth-adjusted multiples useful. Even more, the only multiple that I find useful is EV/Sales/Growth: ~0.65x for the last five years, compared to ~0.39x today (forward multiple), which denotes undervaluation.

PEG would normally be useful as well, however EPS grew from a low base in the last five years (this will push up PEG for the last five years), while today EPS entered an accelerated growth phase just because they have reached a normal point today where business scale leaves its mark (this will pull down PEG today).

For a comparison against peers, I compiled a list of competitors, where I also included Advanced Micro Devices (AMD). While hardly a direct competitor, I like this comparison because Marvell is to Broadcom what AMD is to Nvidia, if we look at market shares:

If we look at the PEG multiple, Marvell seems seriously undervalued, however there are some problems here. Some of these companies have a stronger economic moat (especially Broadcom, maybe Cisco too) so it’s normal to have higher multiples. Some others have a lower moat (like Coherent), so it’s normal to have lower multiples.

Surprisingly, maybe the best comparison could be with AMD, which has about the same profitability profile, growth rates and economic moat, and we can say again that Marvell seems seriously undervalued. However, the key word is “relative”, Marvell is undervalued compared to AMD, so we still have no idea yet if that means undervaluation for Marvell (and how much).

That’s why it’s very important to see absolute valuation, and I use a DCF (Discounted Cash Flow) model. For an optimistic model, my assumptions are:

  • Revenue growth through calendar 2028 according to Table #1 above, then gradually lower growth from 20% in 2029 to 5% in 2034.
  • EBIT margin according to Table #1 above, then gradually increased from 39% in 2029 to 44% in 2034.
  • Tax rate: 15%.
  • CapEx and D&A constant at ~4% of revenues.
  • Growth in perpetuity: 3% (close to GDP growth), corresponding to 11x EV/EBITDA in 2034.
  • WACC: 10.5%, with about 10.9% cost of equity (I use about 11.25% for a no moat name in the IT hardware industry, then I subtract ~0.5 for their narrow economic moat, but I add a little back for their China dependence). This comes very close to Aswath Damodaran’s ~11% for the Semiconductor industry.

Resulting Fair Value is $139.64, about 102% potential upside from ~$69 today (September 17).

For a pessimistic model, I use the following:

  • Revenue growth through calendar 2028 according to Table #2 above, then gradually lowered from 12% in 2029 to 5% in 2034.
  • EBIT margin according to Table #2 above, then gradually increased from 28% in 2029 to 33% in 2034.
  • Tax rate: 19%.

With the other assumptions unchanged, Fair Value is $53.07, about 20% potential downside from the current price (~$69).

By averaging the two, we have a Fair Value estimate of ~$96, about 39% potential upside from the current price. A potential adjustment could come from the relative valuation (EV/Sales/Growth comparison with the last five-years average), which hints to a more severe undervaluation. However, I consider that this is offset by consensus revenue estimates (see Figure #4) coming closer to my pessimistic model.

Risk Management

There are many more bullish strategies besides buying shares, but let’s filter now only the ones suitable for about 40% undervaluation and about 20%+ growth and let's try to find the best risk/reward balance:

Short naked Put

This is a neutral or bullish strategy with capped upside; however, I need to set aside enough cash for a stock market crash. I mentioned my pessimistic Fair Value of ~$53, but the stock can always go even lower temporarily, I really need to consider a worst-case scenario, even if not permanent, and I think this could be significantly lower than $53 for such a volatile stock, so I pass.

Short Put spread (short a Put option, long a lower strike Put option)

This is one of my favorite strategies because it’s neutral-to-bullish, and the cost is significantly lower compared to the short Put, due to the added long Put. On the other hand, it still has a capped profit, so when do I choose this instead of buying shares? When its ROI could be higher than the stock upside from the current price to my estimated Fair Value (meaning higher than 39%). More about this below.

Long deep ITM (In-The-Money) Call

This brings leverage. I usually target 2x-3x leverage, but this acts in both directions. I chose this strategy when the extrinsic value of the Call option is very low, but for Marvell a 50 Call expiring March ’26 is valued at ~$2150 (with the stock at ~$69), meaning about $250 of extrinsic value. That means that I pay $250 for this leverage, or that I will lose $250 with the stock flat in March ’26. I consider the extrinsic value too high, so I pass.

Long ATM (At-The-Money) Call

This is a very different strategy than ITM Call because it has a lot of extrinsic value, but it loses this value very slowly, so it's highly affected by IV (Implied Volatility). I buy it only when IV is very low, because this keeps the Call price low, with the intention of selling it when IV is very high, for example, just before an earnings report (or if the stock jumped in the meantime). But today IV Percentile is 52% for Marvell, so I pass.

The only strategy that qualifies is short Put spread. I usually choose around 6 months (+/- 2 months) expiration date, because a shorter expiration would give me a low premium. Especially if that expiration is before earnings, IV is very low, so the premium collected for the Put spread will be very low.

But how do I choose strike prices? I look again at my pessimistic Fair Value estimate of $53 and I want to choose the strike prices around it, because while this strategy is great with stock up or flat, or even slightly down, it’s not with the stock significantly down. For example, for a short Put 60/50 Put Spread Mar’26, we have:

With Marvell at $53, the strategy would lose about 59% at expiration, but only ~43% in December (see Figure #6), because the long 55 Put still has plenty of extrinsic value. So, I would close the position at $53 and then roll it over eventually by adding a little more funds. However, a maximum ROI of ~33% is lower than my target of 39%. Let aside the fact that I usually close these positions before expiration even with the stock up, to avoid a price reversal, so ROI will be somewhere around 30%.

What if I chose a 60/55 Put spread? Yes, maximum ROI will jump to about 40% because the premium got from the spread relative to the spread cost is higher. But the problem here is that if the stock goes towards that $53 level, both Puts will be ITM, and the strategy will incur a 100% loss at expiration, or about 55%-60% estimated loss in December.

Therefore, I put on watch the 60/50 Put Spread Mar’25, but only if the stock goes down and my potential ROI goes around or above 39%. Of course, if this happens in one month from now, I will switch to the May expiration.

For now, I just bought some shares today at $68.85, about half-position. As mentioned with other occasions, the weight of a position in my portfolio is given by my estimates for rating and economic moat:

Strong Buy

Buy
