…where 1x = a normal weight position.

Since Marvell is a “Narrow moat” in the “Buy” zone, I think it deserves close to a normal position. However, I started with only 0.5x today and:

  • If the stock drops, I will add another 0.25x or more with this 60/50 Put spread, especially if IV is high, for example, just before earnings (estimated for December 4th), because when IV is high, the premium collected from the spread is higher.
  • If the stock drops and IV is very low, I will buy an ATM Call for at least 0.25x, as mentioned above. I think this is an unlikely scenario, because IV is usually higher when stocks are going down (because the fear is higher).
  • If the stock remains around the same level until earnings, I will just add more shares for another 0.5x.
  • If the stock goes higher, but I still find it undervalued, I will buy more shares, but only 0.25x, I’ll wait for the earnings report for the remaining 0.25x.

Takeaway

Marvell Technology is a leading data infrastructure semiconductor supplier with a narrow economic moat, underpinned by strategic partnerships, like the ones with AWS and Nvidia.

Marvell is targeting significant data center market share growth, from 13% of a $33B TAM in calendar '24 to 20% of a $94B TAM in 2028. This is especially fueled by their custom ASIC and optical DSPs, and supported by heavy R&D investment.

A DCF model with optimistic assumptions (like the above 20% market share from a $94B Data Center TAM) models suggests a Fair Value of $139.64. A pessimistic model (with 13% market share from a $75B Data Center TAM) gives a Fair Value of $53.07. With an average fair value estimate of ~$96, Marvell offers about 39% upside from the current price (~$69).

I bought some shares recently at $68.85, for about a half position, and I'm considering additional exposure through short Put spreads if the stock price declines, or through ATM Calls if IV is very low, or through some more shares.

Editor's Note: This article was submitted as part of Seeking Alpha's Best GARP Idea investment competition, which runs through September 23. With cash prizes, this competition -- open to all analysts -- is one you don't want to miss. If you are interested in becoming an analyst and taking part in the competition, click here to find out more and submit your article today!

data.title: SharkNinja: Above-Average Growth Expected To Continue
data.description:
I'm continuing my coverage of SharkNinja, Inc. (NYSE:SN), which reported a positive Q2 2025 earnings report. The earnings report provides insights for SN's future performance. This article will discuss the outcome of the earnings report, insights into future growth, and updates on valuation & the technical outlook.

Takeaways From SharkNinja's Q2 2025

SharkNinja reported an overall positive Q2 2025 earnings report. SN's net sales increased 16% to $1.4 billion, adjusted EBITDA increased 33% to $223 million, and the gross margin increased 185 basis points to 49%. The company also achieved a 200 basis point decrease in operating expenses.

In addition to the gross margin gain in Q2, SN's operating margin increased from 8.32% in Q2 2024 to 11.67% in Q2 2025. The net income margin increased 5.45% to 9.66% over the same period. This reflects SN's efforts to keep costs contained.

These results show the strong demand that the company is experiencing on a global basis. Sales increased 14% domestically and 20% internationally as compared to Q2 2024. The sales strength was broad across SharkNinja's product categories and geographies. These results also show that SharkNinja is creating innovative & disruptive products that resonate with consumers. The reduction in operating expenses demonstrates SN's efforts at cost discipline.

It is important to note that SharkNinja achieved strong double-digit sales gains for the 1st half of 2025. During the same time, the global end markets that the company operates in actually declined in the low single-digit range. This demonstrates SN's strength as a go-to brand and leader for its product categories.

SharkNinja responded promptly to the effect of tariffs. The company negotiated with suppliers for concessions. SN limited discounting and implemented strategic pricing for its products. SharkNinja stated that the price changes only resulted in minimal declines in demand for its products if any. The company believes that tariffs will be the highest in China. As a result, SN now has 90% of its U.S. volume produced outside of China. The company plans to get this to 100% by the end of 2025.

SharkNinja upgraded its guidance for the full year 2025. The company upgraded its guidance for 2025 net sales to be 13% to 15% up from the previous range of 11% to 13%. SN also increased its range for adjusted EBITDA to increase 16% to 18% to $1.1 billion to $1.12 billion. This is up from the previous adjusted EBITDA guidance for a 15% to 17% increase to $1.09 billion to $1.11 billion. These upgrades demonstrate SN's confidence in its business.

SharkNinja's Growth Strategies

SharkNinja has three main strategies for long-term growth. These strategies are a part of the company's mission statement: "to positively impact people's lives every day in every home around the world." SharkNinja strives to solve problems to please consumers.

The first strategy is to expand into new and adjacent product categories. SN aims to add two new product categories per year. The company introduces 25 new products within that same two-year period.

SN's process of innovating is what enables the company to enter new categories. Multiple decisions and criteria are a part of the process of driving innovation. SharkNinja spent many years developing its innovation process. The company believes that this process can be continuously improved.

SharkNinja believes that it has a strong pipeline as a key differentiator. For example, the company is currently involved in 37 categories in the United States. However, SN only has about 10 categories in France and Germany. Therefore, the company has international growth potential as it adds more categories in these under-tapped markets.

SN's second growth strategy is to grow market share in existing categories. The company targets categories with strong selling prices, healthy gross margins, and marketing efficiencies. SN's ability to choose the right categories is evident since all four of the company's categories (cooking, cleaning, food prep, & beauty/home environment) achieved market share gains in Q2.

SharkNinja pointed out that the cleaning category with its robotics, cordless vacuums, and carpet extraction products achieved sizeable outperformance as compared to the industry during Q2. SN's Ninja Crispy air fryer is an example of a product that leads its market. The company challenges itself to continue to scale up and improve existing products in each category. This mindset helps drive SharkNinja's growth over the long term.

The company's 3rd growth strategy involves growing internationally. SharkNinja tends to launch products in the United States. After experiencing sales success in the U.S., it introduced the products in other countries. SN achieved growth in all of its overseas geographies in Q2. This includes strong trends in Benelux, France, and Central Europe. It also includes a bounce-back of growth in net sales in the United Kingdom.

SharkNinja sees further long-term growth in France and Germany. The company also sees growth potential in Benelux, Poland, and the Nordics. SN is also striving to grow in Latin America. All three of SN's growth strategies can help drive SharkNinja's long-term growth over multiple years.

Valuation

SharkNinja is currently trading at 19.2x expected EPS of $5.95 for 2026. This is a little higher than the sector median forward P/E of 18x. The market is awarding SN a slight premium valuation for the company's above-average growth. SN is expected to grow EPS at 17.5% in 2025 and follow that up with a 16% increase in 2026. As a comparison, the S&P 500 (SPY) is expected to grow earnings at 10.7% in 2025 and 13.7% in 2026, according to analysts.

SharkNinja's strong above-average expected growth can be achieved as the company continues its strong sales growth. SN's revenue growth is expected to grow 15% to $6.36 billion in 2025 and 12% to $7 billion in 2026. Add in the margin expansion that the company has been achieving and the above-average earnings growth looks easily achievable.

If we take a longer-term approach to the valuation by looking at SN's expected EPS growth for 2025 through 2028, we might get a more accurate valuation picture. SN is expected to grow EPS at an average of about 16% annually for the years 2025 through 2028. At the same time, the average P/E ratio for those years is about 18%. This gives SN a PEG ratio of 1.13.

I like using the PEG ratio for the growth companies that I cover since it reflects multiple years of expected earnings gains. The growth stocks that I cover tend to perform well when the PEG ratio is below 2. With SN's PEG closer to one, the stock can continue to move higher as earnings increase over the long term.

Technical Perspective

SharkNinja's daily stock chart above shows that the price dipped from the 52-week high of $128. The dip occurred after the stock moved deep into overbought territory, and a bearish divergence formed. The bearish divergence occurred when the stock price made a higher high from the July peak price to the August peak price. The RSI made a lower high during that same time period. Bearish divergences like this typically result in a decline in the stock, which is what occurred.

The RSI did move into bearish territory below 50. The MACD also moved into bearish territory as the blue MACD line crossed below the red signal line and the histogram bars turned from green to red. Although the RSI & MACD are still in bearish territory, I expect the stock to recover over the long term. This looks like a profit-taking pullback as investors locked in gains near the overbought condition. So, I don't think the pullback will last long, given all the positives that SN is accomplishing.

SharkNinja's next earnings report, scheduled for October 30, 2025, could be the next likely catalyst for the stock. Analysts are expecting revenue of $1.62 billion and EPS of $1.38 for Q3 2025.

Risks for the Stock

Competition can be a risk for SharkNinja. SN competes with various companies in each product category that the company offers. It is possible that one or more of SN's competitors produce products that resonate with consumers in the same categories. More intense competition could take market share away from SharkNinja or make it difficult for the company to increase market share.

SharkNinja needs to continually improve its products and create new ones to remain on the cutting edge of technology in each of its product categories. This effort can increase costs for the research and development of these products. Higher costs could lower SN's margins.

Disruptions in SharkNinja's distribution channels could lead to lower product sales. For example, many of SN's products are sold in various brick-and-mortar retailers. It is possible that one or more of these retailers go out of business and close their stores. This could lower SN's total retail footprint for access to potential customers.

Changes in consumer preferences could lead to a shift away from SharkNinja's products to other options. This could include a shift to lower-cost product options that perform similarly to SN's higher-priced products.

SharkNinja's Long-Term Outlook

SharkNinja's Q2 earnings report revealed the company's continued positive sales and earnings momentum. The company has a solid multi-pronged strategy for long-term future growth. I like SN's long-term above-average projected growth. The valuation looks reasonable enough to allow the stock to grow at an above-average pace. The recent pullback in the stock looks like a buying opportunity for investors with a long-term perspective.

data.title: Cactus: Stable Margins, Expanding Strategy Beyond North America
data.description:
Short-Term Pain, Long-Term Gain

Cactus, Inc. (NYSE:WHD) is feeling the sting from steel tariffs—China imports are way up in cost, and even Vietnam isn’t the bargain it used to be. Margins are under pressure, and while the company’s shifting sourcing helps, it’s not a quick fix. Frac activity is unlikely to see a rebound soon, which means softer revenue in the near term.

Despite the challenges, I maintain the "Hold" call, carrying over from the previous article published in June 2025. On the upside, the Baker Hughes deal opens up international markets and brings in carbon capture and hydrogen business opportunities. I expect the Spoolable Technologies segment to hold up. Capex should remain restrained, which, along with a strong balance sheet, will support growth at an affordable cost. Overall, I see moderate EBITDA growth ahead, but near-term headwinds justify a cautious stance.

Why Do I Keep My Call Unchanged?

I considered WHD a "hold" in my article published on June 5, 2025. I wrote:

Early indicators suggest that Q2 energy activities will remain subdued as operators take a cautious approach following the decline in energy prices and global uncertainty. The tariff rise worsens the situation. To counter the challenges, WHD will fall back on strategic moves, including alternative supply chain sources, new wellhead designs, and increasing production capacity at a few facilities. As a result, its operating margin stabilized in Q1, and the stock outperformed the VanEck Oil Services ETF (OIH) in the past year.

Since then, Cactus has faced near-term margin pressure from tariffs and softer frac rentals, although spoolable pipe growth and international expansion present a better outlook. I see EBITDA trending lower over the next few quarters. Longer term, the SPC deal adds diversification, and CCUS and hydrogen provide diversification opportunities. So, I retain my "hold" take on the stock.

Tariff Impact

Cactus took a hit from the June steel tariff hike. Import costs from China jumped significantly higher (nearly 70%, estimates the company). The company shifted sourcing to the U.S. and other regions, but higher costs cut margins.

Vietnam was supposed to help, but rates may stay near 50%, not 20%. It’s still cheaper than scaling U.S. production, so Cactus will lean on Vietnam. I expect the company’s near-term margins to stay under pressure as the company works with vendors and customers to offset the impact.

Frac Market Outlook

Frac activity has weakened so far in 2025. The frac spread count, according to Primary Vision, is already down 12% from Q2, and WHD’s management doesn’t see a rebound soon. Production is also softening, but not as sharply as completions.

Fewer fracs mean fewer wells moving into production, though some backlog helps cushion the drop. Overall, frac-related work will fall more than drilling through the rest of the year. Production should hold up better, but it still faces pressure.

M&A Plans and Strategy

Cactus plans to buy a majority stake in Baker Hughes’ Surface Pressure Control business, which is performing well. The deal helps diversify exposure beyond North America, adding strength in the Middle East. The deal is expected to close by late 2025 or early 2026.

Cactus's management plans to expand internationally through the Surface Pressure Control deal. This adds diversification and reduces exposure to North American shale cycles. Spoolable pipes are gaining traction, especially in unconventional plays, and should drive incremental revenue. New markets like CCUS (carbon capture, utilization, and storage) and hydrogen offer longer-term upside. The planned acquisition of Surface Pressure Control business adds carbon capture and injection wellhead solutions to its portfolio. In addition to these factors, a strong balance sheet and its capex-light budget should support capital returns and encourage future investments.

Near-Term Outlook

WHD sees softer activity in the near term. Management’s cost cuts and tariff offsetting measures are keeping margins afloat. Management expects Q3 revenue to fall as rig counts and frac rentals decline. It appears the company’s management prefers to sideline equipment instead of pushing into a weak market. The U.S. land rig count is already 5% below Q2 and could soften into Q4. Despite weaker activity, EBITDA margins are holding up thanks to proactive cost moves and sourcing shifts.

In Pressure Control, revenue is anticipated to drop to “mid-to-high single digits” from Q2, while margins hold at 28–30%. Management believes Q2 and Q3 mark the trough unless tariffs rise or activity falls further. In Spoolable Technologies, revenue is expected to decline to "high single digits," but international bookings remain steady.

My Estimates

Over the past 18 quarters, its adjusted EBITDA increased by 9% on average. Over the next few quarters, I expect EBITDA growth to moderate. Growth drivers like spoolable pipes, international expansion, and early CCUS work are promising, yet they won’t fully offset near-term declines. Over the next four quarters, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase by 6%–8%. Given WHD’s trailing-12-month adjusted EBITDA of $339 million, I expect its adjusted EBITDA to increase to ~$363 million in the next four quarters.

Analyzing Q2 Drivers

From Q1 to Q2 (as released in its Q2 2025 earnings on July 30), in Pressure Control, revenue fell 5.5% from Q1 to $180 million, driven by weaker rental demand and a less favorable product mix. Product sales declined less than the U.S. rig count, but overall margins came under pressure. Operating income dropped 22% and margins compressed due to lower leverage, higher tariff-related product costs, and weaker rental contribution. Legal expenses also rose, adding $5.1 million in costs and weighing further on results.

In Spoolable Technologies, revenue rose 3.9%, from Q1 to $96 million, supported by stronger domestic activity. Operating income increased 17.5%, and margins expanded 340 basis points. The improvement came from better operating leverage and efficiency gains in manufacturing. Recent investments in the segment contributed to higher profitability.

Cash Flows and Balance Sheet

Cactus's operating cash flow fell 24% in the first half of 2025, driven by softer revenue trends and margin pressure. Free cash flow declined even more sharply—down 30% year-over-year. In response, management trimmed its full-year capex guidance to $40–$45 million, prioritizing investments in Vietnam and efficiency upgrades at Baytown. As of June 30, the company reported $628 million in total liquidity, combining cash and available credit. With zero debt on the balance sheet, WHD’s balance sheet remains robust.

Relative Valuation and Target Price

WHD's forward EV/EBITDA multiple vs. the current multiple is expected to expand, vs. a slight contraction for its peers. This usually results in a lower EV/EBITDA multiple compared to its peers. However, its EV/EBITDA multiple (7.6x) is higher than its peers' (HP, THNPY, and NOV) average of 6.2x. So, the stock is relatively overvalued.

WHD's five-year average EV/EBITDA multiple is 14.1x. If this trades at that multiple, the stock price can rise by 94% from the current level. If it trades at the peers' average, the stock price can decrease by 6%. Since my last publication in June 2025, when I suggested a "Hold," the stock has decreased by approximately 11%.

As I discussed earlier in the article, I expect 6%- 8% adjusted EBITDA growth in the next four quarters. Feeding these values into the EV calculation and applying the current EV/EBITDA multiple of 7.6x, I think the stock should trade between $47 and $48, implying a 17% upside.

Wall Street Rating

Four sell-side analysts rated WHD a "buy" (including a "strong buy"), three analysts rated it a "hold," and one rated it a "sell." The consensus target price is $50.7, suggesting a 23% upside at the current price. Given the company's operational recovery, I think Wall Street analysts are realistic about their expectations.

What's The Take On WHD?

The steel tariff has adversely affected WHD’s operating margin. To mitigate its impact, the company is trying to increase its share of sourcing from the US and Vietnam. Its capex is focused on Vietnam and key efficiency projects. In the completion market, frac activity is slowing, which translates into weaker Pressure Control demand. However, energy production has remained unaffected. Cactus has improved efficiency, which has resulted in better performance for Spoolable Technologies.

The company is growing internationally through the Baker Hughes deal. The acquisition has broadened its Middle East exposure and added CCUS wellhead solutions. I expect these operations to drive a part of the company’s future growth. Cash and liquidity are strong. Despite the headwinds, I expect margins to hold with cost control. The stock is relatively overvalued compared to its peers. Weighing all the factors, I recommend a "hold."

data.title: AMD's AI Ascent: Why Market Share Gains And A Strategic AI Focus Point To Long-Term Growth
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I am bullish on Advanced Micro Devices’ (NASDAQ:AMD) stock moving forward. This is primarily driven by its focus on and growth in artificial intelligence, data centers, and significant market share gains in the desktop CPU segment. Despite NVIDIA’s (NVDA) dominance in the AI market, AMD’s diversified strategy with its new chips, which emphasizes AI inference and an open-source software ecosystem, positions it advantageously relative to its major competitors. In particular, its EPYC, Ryzen, and Instinct lines add to its ability to capture market share from Intel (INTC) and NVIDIA.

An Overview of AMD

What I see as AMD’s core strategy revolves around continuing its aggressive push to extend and expand its product portfolio. More specifically, the company is interested in enhancing performance and energy efficiency across its EPYC, Ryzen, and Radeon processors.

While AMD has placed an emphasis on its AI accelerators, it is also building AI capabilities across most of its product portfolio. By ensuring all of its CPU, GPU, and adaptive computing products are AI-capable, the company is betting on a company-wide AI approach. In particular, these features will be integrated from data centers to everyday computers. A key part of this approach is expanding its software ecosystem. While NVIDIA has opted for a closed-source ecosystem, I’m confident that AMD’s open-source ROCm ecosystem has the potential to appeal to more developers, cloud providers, and even cost-conscious businesses.

In my view, AMD’s strategy of targeting both CPUs and AI GPUs lets it separate itself from its main rivals and secure contracts with hyperscalers and cloud providers. The efficiency and performance improvements its Zen 5 architecture provides position it to continue capturing more of the market.

While NVIDIA is focusing on AI training, AMD has adopted a slightly different approach by concentrating on inference workloads. This market has tremendous room for growth as AI models become increasingly widespread. AMD’s efforts at reducing the cost per token make it an attractive option for businesses needing to deploy models in a scalable and affordable way.

Catalysts for AMD

I see 2024 as the year that marked a new growth trajectory for AMD as it saw returns on the investments it had made across its various segments. This trend looks to continue as the company plans to scale its data center segment rapidly.

AI Ramp-Up

I feel that AMD is positioned for significant growth in the second half of 2025. The ramp-up of its Instinct MI350 series is a major contributing factor to this growth. Furthermore, its AI-related revenue is expected to surge to $9.5 billion in 2025. This growth is being fueled by close partnerships forming due to the anticipation of the company’s upcoming line of chips. In particular, its upcoming MI450 GPU has generated excitement from OpenAI.

CPU Market Share Gains

AMD is continuing to grow its share of the desktop CPU market. In the second quarter of 2025, its share reached 32.2%, which was a 9.2% gain from the previous year. Revenue from its desktop CPU segment reached 39.3%, representing a 20% increase from the previous year. The company has also set a record-high average selling price for its desktop CPUs. The X3D versions of its Ryzen 9000 series have been a key contributor to this surge in average selling price.

AMD’s Acquisitions and Restructuring

AMD has been making aggressive moves to complete acquisitions. In 2024, the company acquired Silo AI Oy, and it has a pending acquisition of ZT Group for $4.9 billion. In addition to the company making these aggressive moves, it is also working to restructure itself in order to improve efficiency. This included a 4% workforce reduction along with channeling more resources into its AI and enterprise segments.

Comparing AMD to Its Peers

Against its peers, AMD has a surprisingly strong position. While Intel still holds a significant lead in the CPU market, I see AMD rapidly closing the gap. This is particularly true for its share of the desktop and server markets. Intel has struggled with its premium Core Ultra 200 series, and it still has an extremely small discrete GPU market share. Despite its struggles, Intel remains a formidable threat as it outspends NVIDIA by 28% and AMD by 156% in research and development.

NVIDIA continues to dominate the discrete GPU market with its commanding 94% market share. This dwarfs AMD’s 6% and represents a sharp decline for AMD, as it held 12% of the market in 2024. To counter this dominance, AMD has adopted the approach of pricing its GPUs 20% to 30% lower than NVIDIA’s equivalents.

AMD’s Financial Standing

AMD’s strong and sustained revenue growth is the foundation of my bullish thesis. The company’s second-quarter earnings help to reinforce this. For the quarter, AMD posted revenue of $7.69 billion, which is a record for the company. This also beat the consensus estimates by $255.65 million. Much of this strength is attributable to the robust demand for its Zen 5-based Ryzen processors.

Looking ahead, AMD’s segments focusing on data centers are poised to be the primary drivers of future growth. While data center revenue grew 14% year-over-year for the second quarter of 2025, I anticipate a much more substantial increase. The strong ramp-up of the Instinct MI350 series and the 2026 launch of the next-generation MI450 position AMD to capture a larger share of the AI market. The combined forces of product launches and market share gains could push 2025 revenue to approximately $33 billion, based on its strong second quarter performance and the $8.72 billion estimate for the third quarter. With its current growth, it is not difficult to see AMD potentially hitting $40 billion in 2026.

AMD’s profitability is a testament to its successful shift toward higher-margin products. The company’s current gross profit margin for the trailing twelve months is an impressive 50.99%. As the company’s high-margin data center segment grows, I believe that there is room for this margin to further expand.

To help model the value of the company, I can assume a net income margin of 9.57% for the trailing twelve months, and an expansion to 12% for 2025 and 15% in 2026 seems reasonable. I believe this is reasonable due to the benefits AMD will enjoy from an improved product mix and its economies of scale. With a revenue of $33 billion in 2025, a 12% net income margin would yield a net income of around $3.96 billion. When dividing this by the outstanding 1.622 billion shares, I arrive at an EPS estimate of $2.44 for 2025. During the 2026 period, $40 billion in revenue with a 15% net margin would produce $6 billion and an EPS of $3.70.

A Deeper Dive Into AMD

When I look at AMD, I see a company with a solid foundation for growth. Its income statement shows that its total revenue for the previous twelve months in December 2024 was $29.6 billion, representing a sizable increase from its $25.78 billion in December 2023.

The company’s profitability was healthy as it produced a robust gross profit of $15.09 billion. However, the company does have significant operating expenses, with much of this concentrated in research and development. R&D has a trailing twelve-month value of $6.97 billion, while the company’s general and administrative expenses are at $3.36 billion.

In terms of its balance sheet, AMD is also in a healthy position. Its current assets for the period stood at $24.51 billion, with cash and short-term investments amounting to $5.86 billion. For the period, its cash flow from operating activities was $2.83 billion. It is also important to look at the company’s book value per share, which stood at $36.78. To me, these factors help to indicate a healthy company with a robust balance sheet.

AMD’s Valuation

Valuation Multiples

A review of valuation multiples can help us come to a price target for the company. Currently, AMD’s trailing twelve-month GAAP P/E ratio is rather high at 93.31. However, its forward non-GAAP P/E is a more reasonable 40.00. I find this difference to be significant, as it shows that investors are far more focused on the company’s future growth. While this ratio still represents a premium valuation, I believe that it is justifiable for a high-growth company like AMD.

The company’s high-end earnings per share estimate for 2027 is $8.50, and applying a 45x multiple yields a potential price target of $382. This is a notably bullish case for AMD. However, the company’s trajectory gives it a strong chance of hitting the upper end of its estimated EPS. The 45x multiple is higher than the sector median of 29x, but this is a realistic assumption given that AMD is an above-average company in its industry.

Price-to-Book Analysis

Using the price-to-book ratio helps me to determine whether the company is being valued at a premium or a discount because it considers the total value of its assets minus its liabilities. In this regard, AMD’s forward-looking P/B ratio is 4.24, which is slightly lower than its TTM P/B of 4.36. This helps to confirm the view that the market is valuing it as a premium.

A comparison of the P/B ratio of its peers is also enlightening. For example, NVIDIA’s forward P/B is significantly higher at 28.77. Additionally, Broadcom’s 22.01 is also far higher than AMD’s. Together, these comparisons make me wonder whether the market is undervaluing AMD compared to its major peers. This further reaffirms my bullish belief that there is substantial upside to the stock over the next year or two.

Risks to AMD’s Bullish Outlook

Intense Competition from Industry Giants

A major concern that I have over AMD’s bullish future stems from the intense competition it will face. As the company seeks to continue expanding, it will have to effectively contend with the dominance of Intel in CPUs and NVIDIA in GPUs. With Intel holding 80% of the mobile CPU market and NVIDIA holding 94% of the GPU market, I am concerned about how much market share AMD will be able to capture. The large research and development budgets of these rivals, along with their institutional expertise, could allow them to stay ahead of AMD’s performance. If this were to occur, it could force AMD to squeeze its margins to remain a viable competitor.

Geopolitical Disruptions

Another primary threat to my thesis is geopolitical and supply chain challenges. The US export restrictions on advanced AI chips are already having major impacts on AMD, including an $800 million inventory write-down. This shows AMD’s vulnerabilities to global politics. This is particularly acute with its Instinct MI308, as the license for shipping them to China is currently under review. This creates a situation that is quite volatile for AMD. In the absence of a licensing deal, its future growth could be stymied. Conversely, a breakthrough on the restrictions could further drive AMD’s rapid expansion.

Challenges with Product Adoption

Introducing new products and services that have the performance and cost to compete against the established rivals is essential for the bullish outlook. In particular, its ROCm software ecosystem is a crucial part of this strategy. This ecosystem is designed to be an open-source competitor to the CUDA system backed by NVIDIA. Without effective customer adoption of this system, a key source of growth for AMD could be hampered. As a result, this is one area of AMD’s strategy that I will be watching closely.

Conclusion

I find that AMD is in a strong position to capitalize on the demand surge for AI and data center solutions. Even though NVIDIA and Intel remain formidable threats, I believe that AMD’s strategic focus on AI inference and its open-source software ecosystem give it a viable path toward substantial growth. Furthermore, its gains in the desktop and server CPU markets provide the company with momentum and capital to continue heavy R&D spending on its AI products. This roadmap provides AMD with substantial upside despite the challenges and risks that it will have to overcome.

data.title: CDZ:CA: This Idea Doesn't Fit In The Market
data.description:
What is CDZ:CA?

The iShares S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Index ETF (CDZ:CA) is a passively managed exchange-traded fund (also known as an ETF) with a NAV of ~$1Bn CAD that invests in stocks designed to provide exposure to a portfolio of Canadian dividend-paying stocks. BlackRock, and most of the other big Canadian banks/fund managers, have multiple ETF offerings that end up providing various investment portfolios that track very similar metrics. While CDZ:CA is somewhat liquid and has an easily verifiable portfolio and mandate, this article seeks to compare this offering with some other passively managed offerings of Canadian equity portfolios because they are often so similar and not worth the high fees.

CDZ:CA's benchmark target is the S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Total Return Index (CAD), and after further review, I believe that CDZ:CA is not worth the investment. The fund is undifferentiated from other passively managed equity funds in Canada and charges higher fees than other ETF peers, like iShares S&P/TSX 60 Index ETF (XIU:CA), another passive TSX 60 ETF with a similar profile, or even XDV:CA, which I just wrote about recently, noting that it's another undifferentiated ETF with higher fees. If investors want to get exposure to higher dividend-paying Canadian stocks, they can easily buy these other ETFs or recreate the portfolio CDZ:CA has, since the portfolio is mostly weighted in about 10 easily identifiable large-cap names on the TSX.

Fund Breakdown

CDZ:CA was launched on September 8, 2006, by BlackRock with a plan to create a diversified, easily tradable fund that replicates exposure to the largest dividend growers in the Canadian market. The S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Index was created by S&P Dow Jones Indices LLC and consists of stocks issued by Canadian issuers who have increased dividends in at least four of the past five years. A company can maintain its dividend rate for a max of two consecutive years over a five-year time frame and still remain eligible. While in theory, the ETF sounds like a good way to lower equity risk with dividend aristocrats, the reality is that the fund has been a dud. The Canadian market is already very mature, with the large-cap index paying an average yield of about 2.5% anyway.

BlackRock is trying to sell investors on a dividend strategy, but in Canada, it doesn't really make sense – and history has shown it to be an underperformer. Below is the returns chart comparing CDZ:CA to XIU:CA over the past 3 months as the market has ripped higher over the summer. XIU:CA, the TSX 60 passive ETF with similar characteristics, has significantly outperformed CDZ:CA.

Because the largest dividend payers tend to be the banks and telecoms, all these funds have similar results anyway because Canada is simply not that big of a market. You don't need dividend strategies because the few big players within banks, insurance, resources, telecom, and real estate all pay ~2-6% dividends.

Additionally, CDZ:CA has an expense ratio of 0.66%, much higher than XIU:CA at 0.18% and XDV:CA at 0.55%. The only rationale for buying into the fund is if the investor is interested in the corporate class as a whole. CDZ:CA is a corporate class fund, and corporate class funds are essentially mutual funds structured as different share classes under a single corporation. That means that there are over a dozen funds in a pool under one entity (i.e., CDZ:CA is the "dividend" focused fund in the class, and there would be other equity and bond funds). The key advantage is in tax efficiency, particularly for Canadian investors in non-registered accounts, because investors can switch between different funds within the same corporate class structure without triggering capital gains. Distributions are often paid out as capital gains or Canadian dividends, both of which are taxed more favorably than interest income. The downside, though, is that corporate class funds typically come with higher management fees, less transparency, and tend to underperform other liquid ETFs. Sure, you can save some money on taxes, but if the fund is underperforming anyway... what's the point? Meanwhile, non-class ETFs are easier to trade, are more cost-efficient, and available in a wide range of sectors and strategies.

Another reason to avoid chasing this fund strategy is due to the index itself. The S&P/TSX Canadian Dividend Aristocrats Index is designed to measure the performance of companies included in Canada that have followed a policy of stable or increased dividends every year for at least five years. However, upon a deep dive into the benchmark, a stock can maintain their dividend for up to two years within that period... so the stocks don't really need to be dividend aristocrats, do they?

Performance aside, I think this fund is charging too high a fee for such a simple portfolio calculation. In terms of liquidity, CDZ:CA trades about 12,500 shares per day worth ~$500K; not ideal for whales. This is an important note, as many funds in Canada, given the lack of institutional and retail ownership, are thinly traded, leading to higher bid/ask spreads and mispricing the ETF units against the underlying holdings. In conclusion, the theory behind the fund isn't bad, but it's unnecessary given the stock underperformance (even accounting for the marginally higher yield vs. passive Canadian equity index peers) and the bevy of high dividend-paying stocks in Canada.

Previous Results & Fund Portfolio

When comparing CDZ:CA's portfolio, there are minor differences in the portfolio breakdown to the main indexes. As of Sep. 16, 2025, the portfolio resembles XIU:CA, with almost identical sector exposures, other than a slightly lower weighting in financials, with the difference in REITs. The bottom line is... is this fund worth paying 3-6x the fees for underperformance, marginally lower beta, and a mediocre Sharpe ratio? I don't think so.

Risks

From a risk perspective, a beta of 0.84 means that the fund will have slightly less volatility than the benchmark - and that has borne out in past results and makes sense given dividend payers are generally more mature companies. The ETF has a Sharpe ratio of 0.79, which measures risk-adjusted return, helping investors determine if they are being fairly compensated for the level of risk they are taking. The formula is the portfolio return minus the risk-free return, divided by the standard deviation. Essentially, it tracks the return required by investors for the risk they are taking, and it should be above 1 to indicate acceptable returns for the risk taken. The higher, the better, and unfortunately 0.79 isn't that good - or at least not good enough to justify triple the fees while other passive ETFs have outperformed in Canada.

Another risk worth mentioning is the tariff uncertainty, which is somewhat mitigated with the USMCA trade deal remaining intact. While companies have eaten a good chunk of the tariffs over the past few months, no matter how you slice it, corporate earnings or higher inflation will emerge, and extended tariffs should lead to a material dip in the market, prompting potential dividend cuts along with stock price drops. While the Canadian market has held up for now, it's worth monitoring. With rates in Canada already low, it's tough to see a clear path forward without some pain, given our intertwined economies with the U.S.

Bottom Line

Ultimately, the bottom line is that this fund is not really worth an investment. The fund has a higher expense ratio and has underperformed peers, doesn't have a ton of liquidity, and has minimal differentiation. The Canadian market has many large-cap dividend payers - stick to buying the main index ETFs to ensure balanced outperformance in Canadian equity... and if you really like dividend stocks, you can easily buy XIU:CA and add a BCE or ENB on your own... even though they are already in the broad market ETFs anyway.
